root.narration: You're the coach. Your team leads 5-4 heading into the bottom of the sixth inning (the final inning in your league). Your left fielder has been great at the plate today (2-for-3 with a double) but he's your weakest defensive player -- he's already misplayed two fly balls, and one turned into a run. Your bench has a kid who's an excellent outfielder with a strong arm but hasn't had an at-bat today. The game is almost over and you need to protect this one-run lead. What do you do?

choices.text: Make the defensive substitution. Put the better fielder in left field. The lead matters more than the bat right now -- we need defense.
Keep the hitter in. He's been hot at the plate, and if we need another run, I want his bat in the lineup. He can handle the outfield.
Move the weak fielder to a less important defensive position like first base, and shift someone else to left field.

decision-after-sub.narration: You make the substitution. Your defensive specialist jogs out to left field, puts on the glove, and pounds the pocket. Two batters in, the opposing team has a runner on first with one out. The batter smacks a sinking line drive to left field -- the exact kind of play your previous left fielder has been mishandling all day. Your defensive sub charges in and makes a beautiful sliding catch! OUT! But the runner on first tagged and is trying to advance to second. Your left fielder pops up -- does he throw?

choices.text: Fire to second! His arm is strong enough to double the runner off!
Throw back to first! The runner left early -- appeal the tag-up!
Hold the ball. The catch was the important play -- don't risk a bad throw.

outcome-double-off-great.narration: Your defensive sub pops up from the sliding catch and fires a laser to second base! The runner was halfway to second, assuming the ball would drop. The shortstop catches the throw and tags the runner sliding back -- OUT! DOUBLE PLAY! Inning over! Your defensive substitution just saved the game! The play that your previous left fielder would have mishandled turned into a game-ending double play because you put the right defender in the right spot at the right time!

outcome.headline: DOUBLE PLAY! Defensive Sub Saves the Game!
outcome.explanation: This is why defensive substitutions exist. In late-game, close-game situations, DEFENSE wins. Here's the math: 1) You're up by one run, so the other team needs to score. Every defensive play you make prevents that. 2) Your weak fielder had already cost you a run on a misplay. In the final inning, another error could tie or lose the game. 3) The defensive sub made two plays in one: the sliding catch AND the throw to double off the runner. That's a three-out swing (from potential rally to inning over). 4) You didn't need the bat anymore -- you were playing defense. Put your best defenders in when defense matters most.
outcome.whatToRemember: In late innings with a lead, defense is more important than offense. Don't be afraid to sub in a better fielder. The bat you remove doesn't matter if you never bat again.

outcome-appeal-good.narration: Your left fielder throws back to first for the appeal. The umpire rules the runner DID tag up properly -- no out on the appeal. Runner moves to second. Two outs now with a runner on second. Still a good position -- the catch was clutch. The appeal didn't work this time, but it showed awareness of the rules.

outcome.headline: Appeal Denied. Runner Advances to Second.
outcome.explanation: The appeal is a heads-up play -- if the runner had left early, that's an automatic out. Smart to try it. But the better play was to throw to second to prevent the advance. With two outs and a runner on second, a single can score the tying run. If you'd thrown to second immediately after the catch, the strong-armed defensive sub could have kept the runner at first. In close games, every base matters. The appeal was a good thought, but the throw to second was the higher-percentage play to keep the runner closer to first base.
outcome.whatToRemember: After a caught fly ball, decide quickly: throw to the next base to prevent the advance, or throw back for an appeal. The advance play is usually higher percentage unless you clearly saw the runner leave early.

outcome-hold-okay.narration: Your left fielder holds the ball after the catch. The runner advances to second without a throw. Two outs, runner on second. The catch was the big play, and you didn't risk a throwing error. Safe approach, but the runner is now in scoring position when he could have been held at first or even doubled off with a strong throw.

outcome.headline: Runner Advances. No Throw Attempt.
outcome.explanation: The catch was excellent, but you left an opportunity on the table. You subbed in this player specifically because of his strong arm -- now is the time to use it! After a running catch, a strong throw to second can either double off the runner or at least keep him at first. Holding the ball is the safest play, but in a one-run game in the final inning, keeping a runner off second base is worth the risk of a throw. You brought in the defensive specialist for moments exactly like this. Let him use all his tools.
outcome.whatToRemember: After a catch with a runner on base, use your arm! A strong throw can double off a runner or prevent an advance. Don't hold the ball when you have the arm to make the play.

outcome-keep-hitter-bad.narration: You keep your hot hitter in left field. Second batter of the inning hits a fly ball to left-center. Your left fielder drifts over, gets under it, and... it pops out of his glove! Error! Runner on second with nobody out. Two batters later, a single to right scores the runner. Tie game. Your left fielder's glove cost you again -- the same mistake he made earlier. The game goes to extra innings, and you're kicking yourself for not making the switch when you had the chance.

outcome.headline: Error! Lead Blown! Should Have Subbed!
outcome.explanation: This is the nightmare scenario. You had a player on your bench who would have caught that ball, but you chose to keep the hot bat in the game. In the final inning with a lead, your team is NOT hitting. You're playing defense. A player's batting average means nothing when they're standing in the outfield. What matters in left field is catching fly balls and throwing accurately -- and your left fielder had already shown twice that he can't do that today. The rule is simple: in late innings with a lead, prioritize defense. You can't score if you're in the field, but you can certainly GIVE UP runs if your defense is leaky.
outcome.whatToRemember: When you're protecting a lead in the final inning, defense comes first. A hot bat doesn't help you in the field. Put your best gloves out there when every out counts.

outcome-move-positions-good.narration: Creative thinking! You move your weak fielder to first base (where fly balls are rare) and shift your first baseman out to left field. Your first baseman makes a decent left fielder -- he catches a fly ball in the third inning. But he's not used to the sun angle and the different reads in left field. A line drive hooks away from him and falls for a double. It's not an error, but a true left fielder would have read it better and made the catch. Runner on second, nobody out.

outcome.headline: Double Falls In. Shuffled Defense Not Ideal.
outcome.explanation: Moving a weak fielder to a 'hiding spot' like first base is a common strategy, but it creates a chain reaction. Now you have someone playing left field who doesn't normally play there. Outfield positions require specific skills: reading the ball off the bat, tracking fly balls, knowing the angles of the wall. A first baseman playing left field is better than a weak fielder, but he's not as good as a true outfielder. The better solution: bring in the defensive specialist from the bench who PLAYS outfield. He knows the position, the reads, and the angles. Don't shuffle players into unfamiliar positions when you have a specialist available.
outcome.whatToRemember: Shuffling players to unfamiliar positions is risky. If you have a defensive specialist on the bench who plays the position, use them. Players perform best at their natural position.
